feed.title: SAP Community - SAP S/4HANA
feed.subtitle: SAP S/4HANA blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Do you know the different capabilities for Contract Management with SAP S4HANA?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Exploring Contract Management in SAP S/4HANA Sourcing and Procurement learning journey.

Overview
This learning journey will provide business users or consultants with an overview of the different capabilities for Contract Management with SAP S4HANA.
You will gain a fundamental overview of the key business processes within Contract Management in SAP S/4 HANA Sourcing and Procurement.

Learning objectives
After completing this learning journey, business users and consultants will be able to:

  • Explain the different solutions provided by SAP for Contract Management.
  • Execute the different business processes in SAP S/4HANA Contract Management

Prerequisites

  • Beginner Training Business Processes in SAP S/4HANA and SAP ARIBA 
    or
  • Exploring the Basics of External Procurement in SAP S/4HANA

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Key User Custom Fields in SAP S/4HANA SAP GUI UIs: How to control field properties (mandatory,...)
entry.content:
Key User Custom Fields in SAP S/4HANA SAP GUI UIs: How to control field properties like mandatory, read-only, and visibility

Do you want to influence the field properties of your key user custom fields in SAP GUI UIs (note that we subsequently use the term custom field instead of key user custom field)? Do you want to configure them as mandatory or read-only or invisible, even dynamically, based on specific business data?

Please note that there is another blog post that introduces the topic of field control for custom fields. It also guides you on how to find out which of these detail blogs you should look at in your specific scenario.
The reason we have several detail blogs is that what you have to do in order to implement field control for custom fields heavily depends on the standard UIs’ implementation technology.
When you already know that in your scenario this technology is SAP GUI, then this blog post is just for you.

This blog post covers the following topics:

  • How to check if your SAP GUI UI supports key user custom fields
  • Whether field control is available for those fields and, if so:
    • How to set your custom fields mandatory or read-only in a static or dynamic way
    • How to set your custom field visible or invisible in a dynamic way

Please note that this blog doesn’t only address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but also SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise.

Let’s assume we want to add custom field to the Change Sales Orders - VA02 app. When you open the app details within the Fiori Apps Library, you’ll find the Application Type field. For this app (VA02) the field shows SAP GUI, which means that it’s a SAP GUI UI.
In order to find out whether this SAP GUI UI supports custom fields, you can have a look at the txt file representing a table attached to this blog (FieldControlSupportByGUITransaction.txt - the txt file is a table where the entries within one row are separated by a tab), which shows all SAP GUI transactions that support custom fields extensibility (this table is taken from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (2408) and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2023). One way to work with this txt file is to open it in excel. The first column shows the Fiori ID of the SAP GUI transaction (in almost all cases identical with the transaction code). The second column shows the SAP GUI context. The third column shows the business context. The fourth column shows whether field control is supported for the given transaction/SAP GUI context/business context, and the fifth column shows the name of the field control BAdI in case it is supported.
A small number of ABAP Web Dynpro-based UIs use exactly the same custom field method to support custom field extensibility. These are also listed in the attached file, and what is described in this blog also applies to them.  
As this table also shows which business contexts are exposed by which SAP GUI contexts, it helps you to verify if the SAP GUI transaction in your situation supports custom fields, for which business context you need to create the field, and which SAP GUI context you have to enable to make it visible in the UI.

For our example for transaction VA02 (Fiori ID VA02) there are two SAP GUI contexts listed:

  1. SD_SALESDOC_GUI: this exposes business context SD_SALESDOC
  2. SD_SALESDOCITEM_GUI: this exposes business context SD_SALESDOCITEM

In the attached file, you can see whether you can implement field control for custom fields enabled for the respective SAP GUI context (we’ll take SD_SALESDOC_GUI as an example).
If this is the case, the column Supports FieldControlSupported shows true. In the column BAdI Name you can see the name of the Cloud BAdI which you must implement get field control for your respective custom field.
In case it turns out that the SAP GUI transaction in your scenario is not custom field-enabled at all, or field control is not supported for the respective SAP GUI context, create a request via the customer influence channel.

You can enable a SAP GUI context for a given custom field by opening the details screen of the custom field in the Custom Fields Fiori app. Within the “User Interfaces” tab,  select “SAP GUI”.
The description of the SAP GUI context describes in almost all cases on which SAP GUI UI this SAP GUI context is exposed. Please note that one SAP GUI context can be exposed in multiple transactions.
In the Excel table attached to this blog, you can check which business context is exposed in which SAP GUI transaction and which respective SAP GUI context you should enable there for the given custom field.
By enabling a SAP GUI context in Fiori app Custom Fields a custom field automatically appears in an UI section of the corresponding transaction. If you want to know how to place a custom field exactly where you want it to be in your SAP GUI transaction, read this blog: . https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-sap/sap-screen-personas-how-to-position-key-user-custom-fields-exactly-where/ba-p/13553723

In some SAP GUI transactions which expose SAP GUI contexts, a BAdI is provided, allowing you to influence the properties of the custom fields dynamically: read-only, mandatory, visible. You can check the Excel table at the end of this blog to find out which of the SAP GUI transactions supports this.

SAP application development uses the BTP ABAP API class CL_CFD_SAP_GUI_CONTEXT_API to support dynamic field control for their SAP GUI contexts. In almost all BAdIs that SAP provides to allow to implement field control for custom fields, the signature of the respective BAdI method is a changing table which has the following columns:

field_name TYPE c LENGTH 30
read_only  TYPE abap_bool.
invisible  TYPE abap_bool.
mandatory  TYPE abap_bool.

By populating this changing table, you can influence the behavior of the respective custom field by setting the read-only/invisible/mandatory flag for the field with the respective field name. Mind that you should not add entries, but only change the property values, as for each custom field in the respective SAP GUI context, a record is already provided.  

Let’s look at a concrete example:
As a prerequisite, we created the custom field “ContractLink” of type “Web Address” using the Custom Fields app on business context SD_SALESDOC and made it visible in the SAP GUI UI “Sales - Sales Order Processing: Change Sales Orders (VA02)” (Fiori Apps Library link). This blog post gives you more information about placing the custom field exactly where you want it to be in your SAP GUI transaction.

When you want to follow the implementation steps described in this blog on your own in an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system, please make sure that at least the following business catalogs are assigned to your user: SAP_SD_BC_SO_PROC_MC, SAP_CORE_BC_EXT_FLD, SAP_CORE_BC_EXT_BLE.

In the Custom Fields app, the field looks like this:

ContractLink custom field in the Custom Fields app

In transaction VA02 (Change Sales Orders) the field looks like this (you can navigate to this screen via Goto --> Header --> Additional Data --> Custom Fields):

ContractLink custom field in VA02 (Change Sales Orders)

Let’s now assume we’d like to make the custom field ContractLink mandatory within the VA02 UI.
The first step is to find a Cloud BAdI (Business Add-In) that allows us to influence the field control information for custom fields in transaction VA02. We can do that by navigating to the Custom Logic Fiori app. In the app, we select Create on the top right corner of the screen.
Within the Business Context drop-down of the search bar, we enter the business context of our field Sales: Business Context. This results in the following list of Cloud BAdIs:

BAdI selection

There, we select the BAdI Custom Field Properties in Sales Document Header as this is the one allowing us to control the field properties of our custom fields.

On the bottom on the left side, we select “Step 2”. Then, we can provide an implementation description and an implementation ID:

Providing implementation ID and description in the Custom Logic app

Then, we select Review and then Create. With that, the BAdI implementation has been created. Before we can create our ABAP coding, we have to publish this BAdI implementation. To do that, we select Publish.
After this has been done, we can click Open Code Editor and with this, we’re navigated to the ABAP editor allowing us to implement our logic.

Implementing the field control logic in the BAdI

On the right side of the editor, we see the signature of the BAdI method. We see our ContractLink field with its technical name YY1_CONTRACTLINK_SDH within the importing parameters.
The next step is to enter our code. We want to make the field mandatory in an unconditional way. We do so by entering the code lines:

read table field_properties with table key field_name = 'YY1_CONTRACTLINK_SDH' reference into data(lr_contractlink_field).
if sy-subrc = 0.
   lr_contractlink_field->mandatory = 'X'.
endif.

Coding of the field control logic in the BAdI

The next step is to validate that

  1. the field is shown as mandatory on the UI
  2. you get an error message in case you don’t give a value for this field and select Save

When you now reopen VA02, we see that the red star for the mandatory field is provided:

ContractLink field shown as mandatory

When you clear the field and try to save it, you immediately get an error:

ContractLink  field mandatory check

It’s exactly the same for the other properties like invisible or read-only. Just set the flag for the respective custom field in the BAdI, and it'll be applied in the UI.

Please note that the signature of the BAdI might be different in other application areas and other SAP GUI UIs. In some cases, it might be necessary to implement two BAdIs: one that takes care that the field is marked as mandatory, and one that provides the check whether the field really is populated. In case of transaction VA02, this is both done via BAdI Custom Field Properties in Sales Document Header.

Now, you are done! Congratulations!
With this, you’re now equipped to

  • find out whether the SAP GUI transaction at hand supports custom fields and
  • if so, whether it supports field control for custom fields and
  • if so, how you can find out the BAdI(s) you must implement to realize the respective field control and
  • you know how to code your field control logic.

Please note that this blog has been elaborated by a team of experts and I want to
thank Yasmina van Rooij(YasminavanRooij), Christian Eckenweber(CE6), Karsten Schaser(Karsten_Schaser),  and Daniel Wachs(DanielWachs) for their extremely helpful contributions.

Feedback, Comments, or Questions:

You’re cordially invited to provide them in the comments section below. Please follow our profiles to get updates when we publish more posts on these topics.
Feel invited to post and answer questions here (https://answers.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26) and read other posts on the topic (https://blogs.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26/).

Other related blog posts:

  1. Custom Fields in S/4HANA: How to control field properties like mandatory, read-only, and visibility
  2. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on SEGW OData Services: How to control field properties
  3. Field Control for Developer Extensibility Custom Fields
  4. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on OData Services exposed with Service Binding
  5. Custom fields in S/4HANA: Key User versus Developer Extensibility
  6. SAP Screen Personas: How to position key user custom fields exactly where you want them to be

entry.title: Custom Fields in S/4HANA: How to control field properties like mandatory, read-only, and visibility
entry.content:
Custom Fields in S/4HANA: How to control field properties like mandatory, read-only, and visibility

Do you want to influence the field properties of your custom fields? Do you want to configure them as mandatory, or read-only, or invisible, even dynamically based on specific business data?

If your answer is yes, then this blog post is just for you.

Please note that this blog doesn't only address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but also SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise.

This blog post covers the following topics:

  • How to set your custom fields mandatory or read-only in a static or dynamic way
  • How to set your custom field visible or invisible in a dynamic way

The answer to these questions differs significantly based on the type of custom field you have at hand:

  1. Key user custom fields: these are custom fields which have been created using the Custom Fields Fiori app
  2. Developer extensibility custom fields: these are custom fields which have been created using ABAP development tools for Eclipse (ADT), either manually, or with the extension fields wizard.

However, this isn't the only dimension that affects how you can implement field control. The second dimension to consider is the UI and OData technology SAP uses to implement the respective standard application.

Below, you can find links to blogs covering the different types of custom fields and applications. They also explain how to find out if field extensibility or field control is supported at all.

  1. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on SEGW OData Services: How to control field properties
  2. Key User Custom Fields in SAP S/4HANA SAP GUI UIs: How to control field properties (mandatory,...)
  3. Field Control for Developer Extensibility Custom Fields
  4. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on OData Services exposed with Service Binding

This blog is intended to help you find the category your specific scenario belongs to and which of the subsequent blogs you should follow.

The simplest way to start is to have a look in the Fiori Apps Library.
Let’s take the example app Manage Sales Orders - Version 2. When you open this link, you’ll find a field called Application Type within the app details start page. In the app, this field is labeled with Transactional (SAP Fiori elements). This shows that it’s not a SAP GUI user interface. Instead, it’s a Fiori application based on an OData service.
An example for Application Type SAP GUI UI is the app Change Sales Orders - VA02. Note that you can find the transaction code of your SAP GUI app in the field Transaction Code within the Fiori Apps Library documentation of the app (in this case, it’s VA02).

In case it’s a Fiori UI, the remaining question is now whether the underlying OData service is a SEGW-based service, or a service binding based OData service.
To find out, open the Fiori Apps Library documentation of the app. Select the IMPLEMENTATION INFORMATION tab and then click Configuration. You’ll find the name of the OData service(s) you’re looking for in the OData Service(s)/OData V4 Service Group(s) area. In our example, this is C_SALESORDERMANAGE_SRV. Now, open ADT (ABAP development tools for Eclipse). Within ADT, search for a service binding with exactly that OData service (in our case C_SALESORDERMANAGE_SRV). If you find it, this means that it’s a service binding or service definition-based OData service. If you don’t find the OData service in ADT it means that it’s a SEGW-based OData service.

Now you know which category your application belongs to and which blog you should have a look at to see whether field control for custom fields is supported, and how you can implement it. The following visualization shows the investigation steps described before.

 

Steps how to find out to which application type the SAP standard application belongs to

Please note that in one of the future releases, SAP will offer a generative AI-driven Extensibility AI Assistant which helps you find out whether a given app supports field extensibility, and whether field control is supported. It’ll also explain what you have to do in order to implement that.

Please note that this blog has been elaborated by a team of experts and I want to
thank Yasmina van Rooij(YasminavanRooij), Christian Eckenweber(CE6), Karsten Schaser(Karsten_Schaser),  and Daniel Wachs(DanielWachs) for their extremely helpful contributions.

Feedback, Comments, or Questions:

You’re cordially invited to provide feedback, comments, or questions in the comments section below. Please follow our profiles to get updates when we publish more posts on these topics.
Feel invited to post and answer questions here (https://answers.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26) and read other posts on the topic (https://blogs.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26/).

Other related blog posts:

  1. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on SEGW OData Services: How to control field properties
  2. Key User Custom Fields in SAP S/4HANA SAP GUI UIs: How to control field properties (mandatory,...)
  3. Field Control for Developer Extensibility Custom Fields
  4. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on OData Services exposed with Service Binding
  5. How to implement a custom field of type “Code List based on CDS view” with a developer extensibility custom entity:  https://blogs.sap.com/2022/07/26/sap-s-4hana-key-user-extensibility-powered-by-embedded-steampunk-custom-field-with-abap-implemented-value-help/
    Also: “How to implement a custom entity in the ABAP RESTful Programming Model using remote function modules”   
  6. How to configure the value help as a context-dependent value help: https://blogs.sap.com/2022/07/27/sap-s-4hana-key-user-extensibility-custom-field-with-abap-context-dependent-value-help/
  7. How to configure intent-based navigation on a key user custom field of type “Code List based on CDS View”: https://blogs.sap.com/2023/06/06/enrich-sap-s-4hana-key-user-custom-fields-with-intent-based-navigation/ 
    and in this case, how to easily find the fitting semantic object and semantic object parameter: Custom field type “Code list based on CDS view”: Finding/defining the right value help view and the right Semantic Object and Semantic Object Parameter for Intent-Based Navigation
  8. Custom fields in S/4HANA: Key User versus Developer Extensibility

entry.title: Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on SEGW OData Services: How to control field properties
entry.content:
Key User Custom Fields in SAP S/4HANA Fiori UIs based on SEGW OData Services: How to control field properties like mandatory, read-only, and visibility

 

Do you want to influence the field properties of your key user custom fields (note that we subsequently use the term custom field instead of key user custom field) in SAP standard UIs based on OData services built with SEGW (Gateway Service Builder)? Do you want to configure them as mandatory or read-only or invisible, even dynamically, based on specific business data? 

Please note that there is another blog post that introduces the topic of field control for custom fields. It also guides you on how to find out which of these detail blogs you should look at in your specific scenario.
The reason we have several detail blogs is that what you have to do in order to implement field control for custom fields heavily depends on the standard UIs’ implementation technology.
When you already know that in your scenario this technology is SEGW, then this blog post is just for you.

This blog post covers the following topics:

  • How you can find out whether the respective application supports custom fields,
  • and whether field control is supported in this case, and if so:
    • How to set your custom fields mandatory or read-only in a static or dynamic way
    • How to set your custom field visible or invisible in a dynamic way

Please note that this blog doesn’t only address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but also SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise.

For some of these custom field-enabled OData service entities, SAP enabled dynamic custom field property control by providing a BAdI (Business Add-In) that you can implement to influence the value of the respective field control field. You can find out whether field control for custom fields is supported for your scenario for the respective OData service entity by having a look at the txt file attached to this blog. The txt file is basically a table where the entries within one row are separated by a tab. One way to work with this txt file is to open it in excel.

  • The first column shows the Fiori ID of the Fiori UI
  • The second column shows the name of the OData service (you find out how you can get the name of this OData service for the given Fiori app in this blog 
  • The third column shows the name of the OData service entity
  • The fourth column shows the name(s) of the exposed business context(s)
  • The fifth column shows  whether field control is supported or not
  • The last column shows the name of the BAdI that you have to implement to implement field control for your custom field

Please note that this table is based on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408 and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2023. Most of the entries are valid for both editions, but not all of them.
In case it turns out that the SEGW OData service-based Fiori UI in your scenario is not custom field-enabled at all, or field control is not supported for the respective SAP GUI context, please create a respective extensibility enabling request via the customer influence channel.

In the section above, the term 'field control field' has been introduced. You may wonder what a field control field is:
Custom fields can be propagated into all OData service entities which have been enabled for key user extensibility by SAP by using the Custom Fields Fiori app.
As soon as a custom field is propagated to an OData service entity, the additional property for the field itself is added. Additionally, a field control field is added, which is referenced by the field property. This field control property shows whether the field should be visible, mandatory, or read-only (you can find detailed information regarding this field control feature here: https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/docs/v2-annotations.html#attribute-sapfield-control ).

The possible values for a field control property are listed here:

Value

Meaning

7

Mandatory - property must contain a value

3

Optional - property may contain a null value

1

Read-only - property cannot be changed

0

Hidden - property should not be visible on user interfaces

 Set the value to “7” to set the field to mandatory or “1” to set the field to read-only. As this is dynamic by nature, you can even set these values depending on the values of fields that are provided such as importing and changing data within the BAdI’s method signature.
Based on such a BAdI implementation, you can influence how and whether the field is rendered in a UI. In most cases, it’s sufficient to implement one BAdI to

  1. make sure that the field gets the red mandatory star on the top right corner of its label in the UI and
  2. to have a runtime check in place to check whether the value of a mandatory key user custom field really has been filled in

Please note that in some cases, application development may have separated both aspects into two different BAdIs. In this case, you have to implement both BAdIs.

The following example shows how to set a custom field to mandatory in case the respective OData service is implemented via SEGW and supports field control for custom fields:

As a prerequisite, we created the custom field JobTitle of type Text using the Custom Fields Fiori app on business context MKT_INTERACTION_CONTACT and made it visible in the Fiori app Contact Profile. The example is taken from SAP Hybris Marketing Cloud.

In the Custom Fields Fiori app, the field looks like this:

JobTitle  custom field in the Custom Fields Fiori app

In the Contact Profile app, the custom field looks like this:

 

JobTitle  custom field in the Contact Profile Fiori app

 Next, implement the field control logic to set the JobTitle custom field to mandatory in this UI. First, we have to search for the BAdI that we have to implement  to influence the field control logic of custom fields in the Contact Profile app. Follow the steps described previously using the txt file attached to this blog. If there is a field control BAdI available for your given OData service and business context, you’ll know that in your case, field control is supported.

Now, open the Custom Logic app (Fiori Apps Library link: https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F6957')/S32) .
In the app, select Create to create a new implementation. Then, search the BAdI in the search field. The BAdI with the description “Field Control of Custom Fields in Contact UI” is found. This is exactly the one we have to implement (see screenshot). In case you don’t have the table at hand that we’ve mentioned before, you can also search for the BAdI using the respective business context.

Select the BAdI and choose “Step 2”.

BAdI selection in the  Custom Logic app

In the next step, enter the implementation ID and the implementation description (see screenshot). Then, click  Review.

Providing implementation ID and description in the  Custom Logic app

On the review screen, you can check what you’ve entered so far. When you’re done, select Create (see screenshot).

BAdI creation in the Custom Logic app

Publish the implementation by clicking Publish. Then, choose Open Code Editor to open the Custom Logic editor (see screenshot).

Implementing the field control logic in the BAdI

Select the changing parameters. Within the FIELDCONTROL_VALUES_CONTACT section, you’ll find the field control field of the JobTitle field, which is the field with the name YY1_JOBTITLE_MPSF. Note that the “F” at the end of the field name shows that this is a field control field that controls the behavior of the field with the field name YY1_JOBTITLE_MPS.
As shown in the screenshot, assign the value “7” (which is the value for mandatory) to this field control field and publish the change. Then, you’re done.

When you open the Contact Profile app UI once again, you’ll see that there is a red star on the top right of the label of the JobTitle field (see screenshot).

JobTitle field shown as mandatory

When you try to save the contact with an empty JobTitle field, you’ll get an error message explaining that saving is not possible as long as the field doesn’t have a value (see screenshot).

JobTitle field mandatory check

For those of you who are interested in more technical details, you can have a look into the metadata of the OData service (URL: /sap/opu/odata/SAP/CUAN_CONTACT_SRV/$metadata). There, you’ll find the following two properties for the EntityType Contact within the entities OData metadata:

  1. <Property Name="YY1_JobTitle_ENH" Type="Edm.String" MaxLength="20" sap:field-control="YY1_JobTitle_ENHF" sap:label="JobTitle" sap:filterable="false" sap:is-extension-field="true"/>
  2. <Property Name="YY1_JobTitle_ENHF" Type="Edm.Byte" sap:visible="false" sap:label="UI Field Control" sap:creatable="false" sap:updatable="false" sap:filterable="false" sap:is-extension-field="true"/>

The first property is the one for the JobTitle custom field itself. You’ll notice that the reference sap:field-control="YY1_JobTitle_ENHF" points to the second one, which is the property for the field control field of the JobTitle custom field.

For every custom field exposed in a SEGW-built OData service entity, there is a field control field.

Now, you are done! Congratulations!
With this, you’re now equipped to

  • find out whether the Fiori app based on a SEGW-built OData service supports custom fields, and
  • if so, whether it supports field control for custom fields and
  • if so, how you can find the BAdI(s) you must implement to realize the respective field control and code your field control logic.

Please note that this blog has been elaborated by a team of experts and I want to
thank Yasmina van Rooij(YasminavanRooij), Christian Eckenweber(CE6), Karsten Schaser(Karsten_Schaser),  and Daniel Wachs(DanielWachs) for their extremely helpful contributions.

Feedback, Comments, or Questions:

You’re cordially invited to provide them in the comments section below. Please follow our profiles to get updates when we publish more posts on these topics.
Feel invited to post and answer questions here (https://answers.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26) and read other posts on the topic (https://blogs.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26/).

Other related blog posts:

  1. Custom Fields in S/4HANA: How to control field properties like mandatory, read-only, and visibility
  2. Key User Custom Fields in SAP S/4HANA SAP GUI UIs: How to control field properties (mandatory,...)
  3. Field Control for Developer Extensibility Custom Fields
  4. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on OData Services exposed with Service Binding
  5. Custom fields in S/4HANA: Key User versus Developer Extensibility
  6. SAP Screen Personas: How to position key user custom fields exactly where you want them to be

entry.title: How to resolve "backend error occurred" in SAP GUI for HTML (WebGUI)
entry.content:
Hi Community,

This blog will help you on how to troubleshoot and resolve backend error issues in SAP GUI for HTML.

While navigating in SAP GUI for HTML or Fiori, you may face "backend error occurred" pop-up and can't proceed with your activity flow. On the contrary, the same scenario works fine with SAP GUI for Windows.

 

  • What is a backend error?

Most of backend errors are self explanatory, the "control not found" for example, means that the control does not exists in a screen when the problematic post request for the control is sent to backend.

"failed to fire action", "failed to set focus" and other errors may also be outlined in the backend error details.

Basically, these are programming errors that needs a correction from WebGUI side, most of them are already resolved in latest Kernel patches.

 

  • How to resolve the backend error?

SAP recommend you to install the latest Kernel SP available as WebGUI corrections are delivered with Kernel patches.

If you cannot update the complete kernel, it is enough to update the ITS related part (webgui corrections) of the kernel using the sapwebgui*.sar file as described in SAP Note 2412840.

 

  • Test yourself

If you have a sandbox system where you can play with, the Kernel can be updated for test purposes, just to see if the issue occurs there. You can find the step by step below:

Easiest way to test SAP GUI for HTML (WebGUI) with latest kernel patch

 

  • and if the issue persist in the Kernel latest patch?

If users are still facing the backend error in the latest Kernel patch, you should open a case with SAP Technical Support with the following information:

  • Exactly step by step to reproduce the problem
  • HTTP traces reproducing the issue (1816543 - How to create HttpWatch trace to troubleshoot ITS related problem)
  • Support communication traces as per KBA 3324689

This will help us to track the problem and provide a correction as soon as possible.

 

All the best,

Lucas Machado

entry.title: Enterprise Management Layer for SAP S/4HANA – What’s New – Release 2023 FPS02
entry.content:
The enterprise management layer for SAP S/4HANA will be available for release 2023 FPS02 with content updates starting February 2025.  

 

This post outlines scope changes compared to the 2023 FPS00/FPS01 versions of the enterprise management layer. Scope items that existed in previous releases might have been updated, for example, due to new functionality being available in 2023 FPS02 or due to new statutory requirements in some countries. These changes are not new Content in Enterprise Management Layer:

New Content in Enterprise Management Layer

New and Deprecated Scope Items in Standard Scope:

 

New scope items (EML only):

AC03

Make-to-Stock: Value flow Produce and Sell Finished Products

OTC30

Internal Commissions Settlement for Sales

 

New scope items (from SAP Best Practices):

1J5

Invoice, Taxes and Complementary Postings (Brazil)

2RP

Electronic Documents (Brazil) (manual setup required)

43D

Integration with External Tax Calculation Engines (Brazil) (manual setup required)

7GB

Service - In-House Repair (was 3XK - In-House Repair)

7GC

Service - Intercompany Execution

7GE

Service - Workforce Procurement

7GD

Service Parts Logistics and Procurement

7IV

Manual Transportation Planning - Basic TM

7IW

Transportation Execution - Basic TM

7IX

Freight Settlement - Basic TM

 

New scope items (from SAP Best Practices, formerly only in EML):

7GT

Quality Inspection of Incoming Material with Multiple Specs (was QM10)

7GU

Stability Studies with Quality Management (was QM20)

 

Renamed scope items:

7GA

Recurring Services (was 4X5 - Recurring Services)

 

No longer available:

QM10

Inspection of Material with Multiple Specifications (now 7GT)

QM20

Stability Studies (now 7GU)

3XK

In-House Repair (now 7GB - Service - In-House Repair)

4X5

Recurring Services (now 7GA)

41V

Presales Management

New and Deprecated Scope Items in Optional Scope:

 

New scope items (from SAP Best Practices):

 

Opt [13] Advanced Shipping and Receiving

7GQ

Basic TM/EWM - Outbound Advanced Shipping and Receiving

7GP

Basic TM/EWM - Inbound Advanced Shipping and Receiving

 

Opt [16] Advanced Transportation Management

6W2

Manual Transportation Planning

6W3

Transportation Execution

6W4

Freight Settlement

 

Opt [17] Extended Service Parts Planning

7JF

Extended Service Parts Planning - Demand Planning

 

No longer available:

 

Opt [06] Group Reporting

4VB

Group Reporting: Consolidation with Multiple Group Currencies

3LX

Group Reporting: Matrix Consolidation

New Scope Items in Service Scope Options:

 

Opt [VP01] - Intercompany Sales and Returns

IC31V

Accelerated Returns to Central Stock with VSiT Replacement

 

Opt [VP02] - Cross-Plant Stock Transfer

SC21

Cross-Plant Stock Transfer with Stock Transport Order Delivery with EWM in Supplying Plant

SC22

Cross-Plant Stock Transfer with Stock Transport Order Delivery with EWM in Supplying and Receiving Plant

entry.title: Do you know the different extensibility approaches for SAP S/4HANA?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Enhancing Key User & Side-by-Side Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA learning journey.

Overview 
This LJ covers two important extensibility approaches for SAP S/4HANA that are available to customers and partners:

  • Key-User extensibility focuses on adaptations created by key users that have a deep business knowledge. Key-User extensibility comprises adding new fields or custom logic, adapting the standard UIs, creating custom UIs, and creating custom business objects.
  • Side-by-side extensibility on SAP Business Technology Platform allows developers to build their own applications, enhancing and supplementing existing SAP S/4HANA (on premise) apps and SAP S/4HANA Cloud apps.

Learning objectives

  • Get to know about the extensibility concept in SAP S/4HANA
  • Personalize the SAP Fiori launchpad
  • Manage the SAP Fiori launchpad content
  • Extend the SAP Fiori launchpad
  • Use variant management
  • Use SAP Fiori runtime authoring
  • Create data source extensions
  • Create custom fields
  • Enable custom database fields for usage in SAP Fiori applications
  • Create custom logic
  • Create custom business objects
  • Create a UI for a custom business object
  • Add custom logic to a custom business object
  • Create custom libraries and methods
  • Create custom code lists
  • Transport extension items
  • Describe essential features of side-by-side extensions
  • Name the most important factors that influence the choice of the extensibility option
  • Develop an extension for SAP S/4HANA with SAP BTP ABAP Environment

Prerequisites

  • Introduction to Application Programming on SAP 

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: SAP Screen Personas 3.0 SP19 enhances usability, adds customization options, streamlines workflows
entry.content:
During our monthly SAP Screen Personas practitioner forum meetings, we listen closely to your feedback and insights. As always, customer requests guide our innovations in SAP Screen Personas, ensuring the tool evolves to meet your specific needs. With SP19, we're thrilled to introduce a set of new features designed to further enhance usability, streamline customization, and support efficient workflows.  

Here’s what’s new in SP19!

  • Use multiple table columns to set up conditional formatting.
  • A new option to disable the software keyboard for the relevant input fields.
  • A new option in the Label control and Button controls that allows you to adjust the icon size. (Available only in the Slipstream Engine)
  • You can now introduce toast messages in scripting  (Available only in the Slipstream Engine)

As a reminder, SAP Screen Personas support now runs for two years for each service pack. So, SP19 will remain in support through November 2026. We are supporting SAP Screen Personas functionality in SAP S/4HANA through at least 2040. If you are on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP Screen Personas capabilities are embedded as Adapt UI for Classic Apps; nothing to install; it’s ready for use.

Use multiple table columns to set up conditional formatting.

Conditional formatting allows you to highlight a row, based on its contents. We created this feature in an early version of SAP Screen Personas in response to a customer request to highlight line items that contained radioactive components as they were subject to additional regulations. Customers also use this for more mundane uses to flag rows that exceed certain values or contain specific text.

With SP19, we add the ability to compare two columns to set the formatting for a row, based on a condition. For example, if the value in Column1 is “Greater Than” the value in Column2, highlight the row in red.

To include a second column in conditional formatting, you use the same rule creation window. The last column now provides a dropdown menu to select a column for comparison, or you can enter a “Comparison value” as before.

A new option to disable the software keyboard for the relevant input fields.

This feature is designed to enhance user experience in applications that rely on automated input methods, such as scanner devices, barcode readers, and RFID readers. The Advanced Property Editor now includes a new Boolean property, hideSoftwareKeyboard, for GuiTextField and GuiCTextField control types. By setting hideSoftwareKeyboard to TRUE, you can prevent the on-screen keyboard from appearing when these fields are selected. This avoids unnecessary interruptions in workflows where physical or automated data entry is used, eliminating the need for the software keyboard.

This property is especially beneficial in high-efficiency environments such as retail, warehousing, and manufacturing, where input fields are populated through scanning instead of manual typing. By minimizing unnecessary distractions and reducing screen clutter, hideSoftwareKeyboard streamlines operations, resulting in a faster and more seamless data entry process. Additionally, it helps maintain a cleaner interface, allowing operators to focus on tasks without extra steps to dismiss the keyboard, ultimately supporting a more efficient and user-friendly workflow.

A new option in the Label control and Button controls that allows you to adjust the icon size. (Available only in the Slipstream Engine)

A new property called iconFontSize is now available forLabel and Button controls having only icon. This editable property enables you to customize the icon's display size directly within the label component, providing greater control over the icon's visual prominence and alignment. By you can enhance readability, emphasize important elements, or ensure consistency across different screen sizes and resolutions. This update is particularly useful for creating more polished and user-friendly interfaces, allowing for finer adjustments that align with the overall design aesthetic.

 

 

You can now introduce toast messages in scripting (Available only in the Slipstream Engine)

A toast message is a small, non-disruptive popup message that gives the user some information and then disappears automatically after a few seconds. In SP19, a new scripting API command has been introduced for displaying non-interactive popup messages similar to the "messageToast" in SAPUI5. Previously, there was no equivalent API in the Screen Personas script editor for creating toast notifications that don’t require user interaction. With this new command, you can now easily implement message toasts for simple scenarios, allowing for streamlined feedback that aligns with SAP Fiori standards. This addition helps enhance user experience by providing subtle, non-disruptive messages in SAP Screen Personas.

Example:  session.utils.showMessageToast("This is a toast message");

SP19 related notes:

  • SP19 release note contains all the kernel pre-requisites: https://me.sap.com/notes/3525171
  •  SP19 client note:  https://me.sap.com/notes/3525180

Your feedback matters!

These updates with SP19 reflect our commitment to helping you use SAP Screen Personas to simplify classic apps to suit the unique demands of your operations, regardless of industry or role. By adding new functionality, improving customization options, and ensuring stability, SP19 enables you to build a more efficient, user-centric SAP experience. We’re excited to see how these enhancements will support your ongoing success and look forward to continuing to bring your ideas to life.

Thank you for your feedback and participation in our forums your insights are invaluable as we strive to make SAP Screen Personas even more powerful and responsive to your needs.

You already own SAP Screen Personas
We still receive questions about whether there’s an additional license fee for SAP Screen Personas. There’s no extra charge—SAP Screen Personas is already included with your ERP license. Thousands of customers are using SAP Screen Personas to streamline screens in their classic apps on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, SAP S/4HANA, and ECC. Changes made with SAP Screen Personas are upgrade-safe, allowing you to build flavors while keeping a clean core.

Learn more about SAP Screen Personas

  • Online training. The best way to learn the basics about SAP Screen Personas (Adapt UI for classic applications) is to take our free training course on the learning.sap.com platform: https://learning.sap.com/courses/adapting-the-ui-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-using-sap-screen-personas
  • Connect with your peers. Every other month, we have a group of customers that gather online to share best practices, demo their flavors, and answer each other’s questions. Experts from the product team also join to explain how to use the latest functionality. If you are interested in joining, you can sign up online to join the SAP Screen Personas practitioner forum. This will add you to the mailing list for the monthly meeting summaries. Please contact “Peter Spielvogel“ or me directly if you want to join the monthly calls.
  • Product documentation. The SAP Screen Personas documentation, recently updated to be even more clear and comprehensive, contains the latest information about the product.

Going forward with SAP Screen Personas

  1. If you are on SP17 or earlier, we recommend you upgrade now, ideally going directly to SP19.
  2. If you are not currently using SAP Screen Personas, why not?
    Worried that we won’t support it into the future? Don’t be.  We are supporting SAP Screen Personas functionality through at least 2040.
    Have other concerns about the roadmap for SAP Screen Personas? See if we answer them in our latest roadmap blog post.the ability to adapt the UI of classic apps is something important to us.
    Have other concerns about the roadmap for SAP Screen Personas? See if we answer them in our latest roadmap blog post.

entry.title: S/4 HANA Cloudification Guide for Developer: Navigating to Released APIs from Classic Objects
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog aims to provide a simple guide for discovering newly released APIs that replace traditional SAP objects, including tables, function modules, class objects, and more.

Whether your organization is implementing Clean Core mindset in S4/HANA On-remise or going through cloud migration(SAP RISE Private Cloud/Public Cloud), one of the first steps towards cloudification is to assess the existing custom solutions are clean core compatible. 

The 3-tier extensibility model is a classification that provides rough idea of how much risk of system upgrade will disrupt your custom solution and how protected and stable it is. In the prerequisite of Tier 1, which by definition has the smallest risk of getting affected by upgrade disruption, states the enforced usage of released APIs. Using released objects is a must and a first step towards clean core way of building custom solutions in your SAP system. 

The challenge with deprecated objects

Before this clean core concept or 3-tier extension model were introduced, the standard way to extend SAP ERP core is using now so-called classic extensibility, which corresponds to the 3rd-tier in the 3tier-extensibility model. Organizations operating with SAP before this new concept are most likely full of classic extensibility solution. If their system is rated with today's clean core standard, the result will probably say it's not cloud ready/compatible. Number of SAP objects used in the classic solution are now labeled as deprecated in the new standard, resulting in renovating all the custom solution that uses the deprecated objects. To adapt to the new standard, it is crucial to map the deprecated objects to the new released APIs to ensure the functionality and the performance of the custom solutions.

(This is, of course, not customer's fault as back then the solutions were probably implemented with the newest technology, insight and standard of that time.)

With this in mind, let's look at what's the easiest way to find released APIs for each classic ABAP objects.

1. Classic objects ➡️ Released objects   

Database Tables

The easiest way is to use Fiori App: View Browser(F2170). Check out ➡️ Finding released CDS views for classic SAP tables  for detailed steps to find released CDS views for classic SAP tables. 

The second easiest way is to use SAP Cloudification Repository, which is also covered in the blog above (however not all objects are listed).

Function Modules / Class

Open the function module in question with ABAP Development Tool(ADT). Open Properties->API State and the successor object is specified.

Example below checks for function module BAPI_PO_CHANGE and class object CL_SD_DOCUMENT_FLOW_RT.

 

 

Alternatively, you can use SAP Cloudification Repository(however not all objects are listed).

 

Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) Definitions 

Open the BAdI Definition in question with ABAP Development Tool(ADT). When opening the object, the enhancement spot that uses the BAdI Definition should open and all the BAdI Definitions will be listed. Right click on the BAdI Definition in question -> API State -> Display Use System-Internally.

Example below checks for BAdI Definition MEOUT_BAPI_CUST.

Release state is shown as well as the successor objects if you click Next.

 

 

 

Function Interfaces, Authorization Object, Data Types..etc.

Successor for rest of objects can be found in ADT same way as function module and class objects.   

 

2. Exploring released APIs

In ABAP Development Tool(ADT)

ADT offers tree views to list all the objects depending on the object release status. You can also customize the tree as you see fit by adding Application Component, Packages and Software Component as an extra layer. It does not only show the released objects but also deprecated objects as well as deprecated objects with successors. 

Check out ➡️ Exploring released APIs in ABAP Development Tool for step-by-step guide. 

 

 

Trees that show Released Objects, Deprecated Objects and Deprecated Objects with Successors

 

In SAP Accelerator Hub

SAP Accelerator Hub is an alternative to find API's for those that don't have developer access and want to explore APIs from business context. It contains not just APIs for tier1/tier2 cloud development, but also out-of-box template for business processes, integration scenarios, events based solutions and everything you need for extension your SAP ERP system. Choose your product(S/4 Public/Private Edition, BTP, etc) and start exploring.

Filter the package and explore released APIs with your desired business area.  

 

Appendix

Released APIs

APIs marked as released are indication that they are technically stable and is the only option available in tier 1 extension to communicate with SAP backend. SAP categorizes the released objects in 5 ways depending on the types and the intension of the extension. In this blog, we focus on the cloudification of SAP objects used for custom development hence will only touch on C1 Contract (Use System-Internally).

  • Extend (C0)
  • Use System-Internally (C1)
  • Use as Remote API (C2)
  • Manage Configuration Content (C3)
  • Use in ABAP-Managed Database Procedures (C4)

C1 Contract (Use System-Internally)

Released in C1 contracts indicate that the object is stable enough to be used in Key-User Extension(Custom fields and Custom Logic apps) and Developer Extensibility(custom solution developed solely by ABAP Development for Cloud language scope). The release state is managed separately for each usage and can be found in ABAP Development Tool.

entry.title: Message no. WR301 "Valuation price of component XXXX in plant YYYY is zero"
entry.content:
During Good Receipt of a prepack article at the Store level leads to an error message 

Valuation price of component XXXX in plant YYYY is zero

Message no. WR301

 

This error message occurs because the component of a prepack at the receiving plant has a Moving Average Price (MAP) of zero. During goods receipt, the system attempts to update the inventory and value of the components. If the MAP is zero, the system triggers this error to prevent incorrect valuations.

Root Cause:

  • When creating or splitting prepack articles, the component's MAP is used in the weighting profile for valuation.
  • A zero MAP leads to an incorrect valuation for the header article.
  • This behavior applies to Display Sets as well and is a standard SAP system process that cannot be modified.

Resolution Steps:

  1. Update Valuation Price:

    • Use transaction MR21 to update the valuation price of the components.
    • Once the MAP has been corrected, the system will allow the goods receipt (GR) to be posted.

  2. Preventative Strategy:

    • Implement a strategy as part of the Master Data setup to ensure components have an MAP during creation.
    • This can be addressed in one of two ways:
      • During Article Master Creation: Ensure MAP values are included when creating articles at the logistics DC or store level.
      • During Listing: Leverage reference handling to ensure MAP values are inherited appropriately during the listing process.

Conclusion:

By ensuring components always have a valid MAP during setup, such errors can be prevented, and smooth processing of goods receipts can be maintained for both prepacks and display sets.

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Support Academy Newsletter December 2024
entry.content:
Dear customers,
Find here an overview of the SAP Enterprise Support Academy monthly newsletter on latest news, training courses, upcoming sessions, and further educational offerings.

This E-learning provides an overview of the SAP Design to Operate lines of business, covering Design, Plan, Manufacture, Deliver, and Operate, and offers a comprehensive understanding of end-to-end supply chain operations.
